speaker: interviewer
text: -- '96, Mette Shayne, tape 2. We left at about 7:00 on October 1 or 2. You're boarding the boat. Tell us what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very bad. It was a very bad night. It was bad weather, and it was also, at some point, that the fishermen apparently really didn't know where they were. There was also a bombardment of northern Germany, the peninsula [INAUDIBLE]. And at one point, we were so close to that that we were not sure, in the morning when we finally saw land, whether it was actually Germany or Sweden. My brother tells how we were all put down in this little-- little cubicle inside the boat. And of course, we had to be very quiet and-- until the boat got out on the water. Then they let some of the men up on the deck. And my father, and my brother, and my uncle were on the deck the rest of the night. And they were completely soaked to the bone and very cold, while down in the-- in the cabin, there were, I don't know, probably 12, 15 people. And everybody was seasick. It was absolutely horrible. And we, as children, were put in the-- in the little bunks. I don't think anybody slept, and everybody was really sea-sick. And there were buckets for people to throw up in, and they were knocked over. It was-- it was really, really bad. But the only thing that we had along-- somebody had brought chocolate along, which does, supposedly, give energy. And of course, there was no chocolate in Denmark, so where he had it from, I don't know. My mother had a flask of brandy. And I especially remember that, because when we landed in Sweden, my brother was passing out. He was so cold and so frozen. And I remember her pouring some brandy in him to try and revive him. When we first came to Sweden, there was a big German ship in the harbor. And we were guided away from that into the harbor. And the Swedes were wonderful in welcoming us. I believe we were the first boat to come to this little, small town, which certainly was not a very direct route. We were considerably further west than what they had planned to-- where they had planned to land. We landed in the town of [INAUDIBLE], and we were taken to the police station and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Customs people. And I remember, my brother was in such bad shape that he could not sign the papers that he had to sign. And he needed to go to the bathroom. He had to ask a soldier to help him unbutton his pants because his hands just were not functioning at all. And then he passed out, and they just could not wake him out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they finally have said that he could come back the next day and sign the papers. And-- and I assume we must have had a cab or whether we walked to the hotel in the town where we then stayed. I think we must have stayed there two nights.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father, right away, took all of our clothes to a cleaner, because it was not very nice after this night. And we always have remember that when he came to pick it up, the cleaner would not take money for it when he realized who we were. And-- and he had stuffed our pockets with candy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- those were the things. My brother also remembers, when we were at the police station, that somebody lowered down the apples for us from-- from an apartment above. I-- I don't remember that, but that's in his-- in the memories that he-- memoirs that he has written. That one, I didn't remember. So my father got very busy making phone calls. It was too early. The Swedes were not so organized yet. But as the next nights, more and more Jews came over, they organized like camps where-- where the Jews would stay the first nights, until they could make arrangements to-- many had relatives in Sweden or had-- had places they could go until they could make arrangements for getting them settled.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some kind of camps. But we were lucky enough not to have to do that, because my father's company, the major part of the stock in my father's company was owned by [INAUDIBLE], which is-- it was a big Swedish-- very big Swedish concern. So the natural thing for us was to go to-- to the town where [INAUDIBLE] was located. And this town, called Vasteras, was like a company town. This company just dominates the town. It dominated the town at that time, anyway. And so my father got in touch with them, and-- and the next day, we went on the train, all the way up to Vasteras, which is about 115 kilometers west of Stockholm. And so we were more luckier than a lot of people, because my father was able to do some work and be in contact with his company during the time that he was there, even if he was-- it was a very frustrating experience for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do in Sweden for the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. I think he was in touch with them. But I think there wasn't that much he could do, so I think he probably-- he went to the office every day. But what he did, I don't really know. He probably--

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was paid a salary at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was paid a salary. Probably, part of the salary from Denmark was being paid to him. during the time that he was there. He also was able to help several of the people from his company, who had fled, to get jobs with this company so that the whole group of people would come to Vasteras. And there were other Danes there that got employment that way, during the time that they were in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- well, yes, those people that came to Vasteras, I believe, were all Jewish. But later on-- it was really until later on that a lot of non-Jews also came with the freedom fighters, who had-- had to leave, and who then, also, were-- were helped with employment.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back, just a moment, to the boat. Do you remember the name of the boat or the name of the-- the person, the fisherman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, according to my father's recollection-- because I certainly didn't remember-- the man who owned the boat was-- his name was Svend Nielsen, which is a very Danish name. And boat was [? KR134. ?] That's all I know. What I do know is that the fishermen, right away, said that they had to turn back right away because there was more work to be done, which meant that they were-- they were going to rescue more people before they were resting, which was wonderful, knowing that they-- what kind of danger. At one point on the trip over, we were trailed by a German patrol ship. And I still think that they must have decided they didn't want to catch us, because I'm sure they could have gone faster than-- than our boat, if they had wanted to. And there's no doubt in my mind that there were German soldiers who decided they were not going to catch these people, because it is such a-- the general stretch-- not where we were, but the general stretches-- should be so easy to survey, that it's hard to believe that many people could have gotten across if the Germans really had made an effort to-- to stop them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you lived in Sweden, in Vasteras, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we first came, we were put up in the-- in the hotel in Vasteras. And we were-- we were there for about less than a week. Actually, the first night, some of the Swedish directors of the company came and-- and had dinner with us. And the-- they put Danish flags on our table, and there was a-- there was a small orchestra, and they played Danish songs for us. Actually, two nights in a row this happened. And then we moved-- after less than a week, I believe, we moved to a smaller and much more modest hotel that actually was owned by the company. And we stayed there until just after the first of the year in 1944. Then my father was able to obtain a small apartment, and we moved into an apartment. And we stayed in this apartment until we left. And it was a two-bedroom apartment. And the company had some furniture that they could lend us, so that we didn't have to buy-- we bought very little furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after we had been there about a week, my mother took me to-- to the local elementary school, and I started school right away. And my brother had a little more difficulty, because he was already in high-- in-- well, in secondary school is what-- I guess what he was. And it was a little harder to fit him in with the Swedish curriculum. But he-- but he also started school. And after a couple of-- I think in less than a month, he was able to move up one grade, because he found that-- in order to fit in better. But he had a harder time because in order to fit in with his friends, when he came home, he would need to pass an exam that was not available there. And just before the end of the war my parents took him to down to Ytterberg, where there was a Danish school. And he was enrolled there for the last couple of months. and had a very hard time trying to catch up. And he had to stay behind when we went home, because they had to finish the exams. But it was good that he did because when they came home, they did let him in with his old classmates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was language a problem for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first the month or two, probably. I mean, we did not know Swedish, but we picked it up very fast and very fluently. And I got very good grades and-- and wrote fluently in Swedish by the time I was leaving again. Unfortunately, I don't remember my Swedish fluently. My brother is terrific-- he still speaks it fluently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to make friends pretty easily?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made-- we had-- we both made friends while we were there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody who you escaped with accompany you to this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the people who ended up in this town were-- there was a distant cousin of my father's and her husband, who was an engineer, that he helped get a job. So they were living there with their children. And also, another older engineer and his wife were living there. And they were kind of the people that we had-- that my parents had social life with during that time. The rest of our family on my mother's side all-- mostly ended up in Ytterberg and outside Ytterberg where part of our family was Swedish. There was a Swedish branch of the family. And they had very big summer houses, and they put those houses at their disposal. So they lived in these big houses the entire time.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you spent in Sweden what did know about the war? How did you get information about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we followed the BBC. And we had a big map up on the wall and moved colored pins every day, when we got the reports, as to how the troops were moving. So we followed it with very keen interest. Once in a while, my father also had-- had contacts with Denmark. And once in a while, there were people from Denmark who came visiting, from the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this something you talked about with your friends-- how the war was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. You mean with the Swedish friends? They obviously did not have the same kind of interest in it or knowledge about it. It-- life there was not very marked by the war. And you could get all the things that you couldn't get in Denmark, like chocolate and-- as when my father went back in the army-- he went back in the Danish army that was-- that was founded in Sweden to-- because they didn't know, at that time, whether they would be going back fighting. He went back as a typical [INAUDIBLE] soldier with his-- all his pockets filled with chocolate. That was handed out and gladly accepted when he-- when he got back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were welcomed nicely in Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we never felt any of the anti-Semitism that-- that many people say is-- is rampant in Sweden. We never felt it. And I think the Danes were-- were welcomed and treated very well. There was some more bitterness from the Norwegians. But they had a much harder time and also, of course, a much more difficult way to get across because of the mountains. And I guess they had problems that we didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended in '45--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war ended, my father was already with-- with this army. And-- and he was-- they transferred over the night-- May 4th was the night that-- the day that the Germans capitulated in Denmark, not-- and Norway. And that night, he was-- he went across. He was a-- he was a truck-- he was too old to be in the army. But he-- he was a truck driver. That, he could handle. And-- and my mother and I were left alone in Vasteras to pack up the apartment, because my brother had already got to Ytterberg to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we packed up as fast as we could. And I just remember my mother getting a phone call through to some of our closest friends in Denmark, and crying on the phone, and-- and-- and my saying to her, afterwards, that I had never seen my mother cry. I remember that very well. And then we went down to Ytterberg to be with the family until we were-- we-- we came home. How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have a date for when we came back. But it must have just taken a couple of weeks, about three weeks, because my brother came back around the 10th of June. And he stayed behind, like, three weeks or so. So we must have come home around the 20th of May. And that jives, because we came back-- it was all very well-organized and-- and-- and we came back through Helsingor. And I remember going on the train from Elsinore down to Copenhagen, and it-- and the-- the beech trees had just opened up, which is a very beautiful sight in Denmark. And I remember my-- being with my grandfather, being very emotional about seeing the Danish trees and the-- and the well-known stretch. And-- but the thing that was so remarkable in Denmark was not only the way we were helped to flee, but the way we were welcomed back. I remember, very vividly, coming to the central station in Copenhagen, and huge throngs of people being there to welcome us, and people with big banners for specific people. And I specifically remember my one cousin-- all of his friends were there with big banners saying, welcome home. That-- that you did not see in other countries. And we came home to a house that, practically, had not been touched. It had been watched over by friends. They had sometimes used it to hide freedom fighters. The Germans had been there. They had knocked a hole with their bayonets, in a-- in one of the squares of wood in the garage, in order to get in, because it was locked, and they couldn't get in. And you could also see bayonets at my father's desk, because it had a-- had a fake drawer. And they were trying to open it. They had taken away some address books from his desk. But otherwise, nothing had been touched in our home, which is remarkable. The-- probably, the silver was put in storage before we left. That, I don't know, remember. But my father had brought-- he had a considerable wine cellar. And he had brought his wines into a friend's house. And this friend was very upset that one of the bottle of brandy had been used, because he had had company. And when he had told them he didn't have any brandy, they had insisted that wasn't true. And they went-- had gone in the basement and found my father's brandy. So they had used his brandy. And my father, of course, could care less. And he wished they had only drunk a lot more of it. That was very minor, but that's incredible. And I remember the feeling of going into my room. I-- you know, I had shared a very small room with my brother. And-- and I went into my own room, and opened my closets, and found toys that I, of course, had forgotten I had. So we were incredibly lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it, going back to your friends and your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems to have been very smooth. I, apparently, had very little trouble fitting back in academically, and continued, very much, the life that-- that we had had before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your grandparents spend the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had only two grandparents living. My grandfather, who was one of the last ones to leave-- he wouldn't leave until he knew that all of his children were safely across. And he was in one-- in the group that went up to the northern part of Denmark and was split in two. And he was hidden in a barn, and the others were hidden on the roof of a church. And the group in the church were taken. And he-- he was not taken, so he was very lucky. I'm trying to think. I believe that-- that he also lived in Ytterberg during the war. And my grandmother on my father's side, she lived near Helsingor, because her brother owned an apartment there and had businesses in Sweden. So she had a-- had an-- he had an apartment where she had her sister stayed, and they did visit us several times during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your father resumed his career when he came back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until he retired, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life fell back into place for you. I want to ask you a question about the Jewish content of your life. I know that you came from an assimilated family. But when these events occurred, and they occurred specifically because of your Judaism, did it cause you to wonder what Judaism was about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no-- it did not really change our life. And in Sweden, I think I mentioned to you, I-- some friends of my parents had given me a candlestick. And not until I moved here with my husband, and he unpacked it-- he said, oh, you have a menorah. And I didn't know what a menorah was. And I said, no, this is a Swedish artifact candlestick. And he said, no, it's a menorah, and it has the Star of David on it. So not until I got a little older, I decided that I should know something about it. And I went to Jewish instruction at the synagogue in Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also mentioned that your father would occasionally go to the synagogue. What was that about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was out of tradition and feeling for his parents. And his parents, again, because they did have a seat at that time-- and I think it still has. You can, like, buy a seat or rent a seat in the synagogue. There's only one synagogue in Denmark. It's orthodox, and the men have seats, in which they put their [INAUDIBLE] and their-- and he had this seat that he had taken over from his father, that he paid for out of reverence to his father. And he would go, like, once a year. But my mother didn't go, and I don't think it meant a great deal to him. But he still had this family feeling. It was more a family feeling, certainly, than a religious feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's flash forward to the postwar years and meeting your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I am-- I had actually been to the States before I met my husband. I-- I-- when I finished my library degree, I came and spent a year in this country and traveled all over, because I thought it was the last time-- the first and last time I was going to be here. And when I came back to Denmark, I met my husband. He was on a Fulbright scholarship studying labor law in Denmark, and we met at a friend's house. And so we were married very briefly after that. And I have been here since 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have three sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three sons, yeah. And we did bring them up knowing they were Jewish, although it's not a religious house. We did-- they did go to religious school for some years. They are not bar mitzvahed, but they-- but they do know-- certainly know where they come from.

speaker: interviewer
text: And two of them are married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two of them are married.

speaker: interviewer
text: No grandchildren yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No grandchildren, and they actually-- my youngest daughter-in-law is [INAUDIBLE] Jewish, and will uphold Jewish traditions. And the oldest one is-- is part Jewish. But she is Jewish in Jewish sense of that her mother and-- from the mother's family. But because she's related to us, so she's a third cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did your life turn out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been very lucky. I have a very good life and a very good husband. And it certainly is because of him that I have learned anything about anything Jewish, because he was brought up in a-- in a fairly traditional-- not-- certainly not religious, but in a traditional Jewish family. So he's taught me everything I know about it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we'll stop now, and we'll look at some family photographs and some of the documents that you have. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this-- this is an ancestor on my mother's side, Ruben Henriques. And he was born in 1771, and I have the genealogy of that whole side of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the 18th century?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a-- a picture of a music conservatory in Copenhagen. And my great-grandmother on my mother's side is the person on the-- on the left, Mina Meyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it painted?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was painted in 1868.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my-- my grandmother, and-- and her mother, and her siblings. And that must've been taken around 1885, 1888.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on my father's side. It's my grandmother on my father's side, who's in the foreground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my parents, Inger and Poul Hannover, and my brother, Allan, and me. It was taken in 1951, celebrating my father's 25th anniversary with the company [INAUDIBLE], of which he was president.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in Copenhagen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house my parents built when I was born. I was born there, and they lived in it until it was demolished to make room for a highway, about 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married there, in the garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a wedding picture. We were married at the July 10, 1960. And my husband's name is David. This is a-- a picture from the pre-nuptial dinner of the wedding of my youngest son. It was taken October 12, on the 95th floor of the Hancock building in Chicago. And to the right is my oldest son, Philip, and his wife, Helen, and then my youngest son, Ralph, and his wife-to-be, [? Lara. ?] And to the left is my middle son, Adam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a postage stamp. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a stamp commemorating the 50th anniversary of the-- of the end of the occupation of Denmark, in May of 1945. And it actually is the boat that we fled on, to Sweden. It was [? KR134, ?] and it is possible that it is my father in front. But the remarkable thing is that the Danish government is commemorating, with this kind of a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's amazing. Thank you for telling your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
